intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users need visible, continuous feedback on their health experiment timeline rather than discrete checkpoints
facade.axisTargeted: Fluid animated transitions vs snappy instant updates
facade.label: Smooth progress bar
facade.acceptImplies: Build animated progress indicators, smooth state changes, and flowing micro-interactions throughout the dashboard
facade.rejectImplies: Favor instant, static displays with minimal animation—numbers and grids update without motion or easing

evidence.hypothesis: Users need visible, continuous feedback on their health experiment timeline rather than discrete checkpoints
evidence.implication: Build animated progress indicators, smooth state changes, and flowing micro-interactions throughout the dashboard

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer conversational language ('How's your energy today?') over clinical language ('Record vital parameters') when logging health data
facade.label: Casual check-in tone
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into warm, conversational microcopy; use 'you' language; celebrate small wins with emoji or gentle humor
facade.rejectImplies: Adopt clinical terminology; prioritize data density over personality; use neutral, instructional tone throughout the interface

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard benefits from calm, desaturated tones that feel medical-adjacent but not sterile—reducing cognitive load during frequent check-ins
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool, calm vs energetic
facade.label: Soft, muted colors
facade.acceptImplies: User values serenity and visual restraint; dashboard should feel like a refuge, not a data command center
facade.rejectImplies: User wants vibrant feedback and energetic visual cues; needs color to feel alive and motivated, not meditative

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer persistent navigation on the left with single-column vertical scrolling over tabbed layouts for scanning long experiment timelines
facade.label: Sidebar + scrolling
facade.acceptImplies: Future probes should test depth of sidebar (wide vs narrow) and infinite scroll vs pagination
facade.rejectImplies: Explore tab-based navigation and horizontal scanning patterns as viable alternatives

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer metric-first displays (large, scannable KPIs) over narrative-led contexts (what the number means)
facade.axisTargeted: Data hierarchy: prominent metrics vs. narrative framing
facade.label: Big number, small story
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should lead with large, bold numbers; context/story comes second or on-demand
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard should nest metrics within narrative explanations; numbers serve the story, not vice versa

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer gentle, approachable visual warmth over clinical sterility, which reduces cognitive load when reviewing personal data.
facade.axisTargeted: Color temperature and emotional tone (warm/inviting vs. cool/clinical)
facade.label: Warm, soft colors
facade.acceptImplies: Pursue warm palettes, soft gradients, and ambient lighting in cards and data visualizations to create a sense of care and comfort.
facade.rejectImplies: Explore cooler, more neutral or high-contrast palettes that feel objective and data-focused rather than emotionally warm.

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users find abrupt screen changes jarring when tracking health data over weeks; gentle, spring-like animations reduce cognitive friction and make long-term tracking feel less clinical
facade.axisTargeted: Fluidity of state changes—snappy instant vs. easing curves with momentum
facade.label: Bouncy transitions
facade.acceptImplies: Invest in spring animations, ease-out timing, and momentum-based scrolling for all dashboard transitions; prioritize feel over speed
facade.rejectImplies: Keep interactions instant and direct; users value speed and clarity over aesthetic flourish; focus on fast feedback loops instead
